segments.text: Hi everybody. My name is Olivier
Gdan. I'm the co-founder of Sonar and
today I'm going to talk about the newly
released capability of architecture in
SonarQube. This presentation is
going to be divided into three parts.
First I'm going to talk about the
problem we are trying to solve.
Second part is going to be about the
solution, what we have implemented in
Sonar to solve this. And the third part
is going to be what's next for you.
So let me start with the problem.
Basically, as you know, the world
operates on software, which means that
software is now the single most
valuable asset for organizations. And
one thing which reduces this value is
actually technical debt, which I think
we have, in the last 17 years, done a
very good job to help organizations
handle. But we have mostly worked at
the code level, which is we have helped
people to work on their maintainability,
reliability, and security
with SonarQube, and we felt it was
time to actually move into more
structural debt, which is about how the
software, how the application, is
organized.
What are the abstractions? What's
the design? Do components have really
single responsibility,
etcetera, etcetera.
And when you do not follow this, it's
actually not that much fun, which is you
can see symptoms like engineers are
leaving because the environment is too
messy. Every change you make has side
effects and they are very difficult to
explain, and overall your velocity is
going to drop.
So these are the symptoms of not
managing architecture. What you should
also know is that it doesn't come
suddenly. This is very sneaky, the way
it arises, and it's very much an
erosion, which is generally at software
creation, application creation, there
is no real problem. People thought
about what they want to build.
They organize it well,
and it's okay.
But little by little, when you make
shortcuts during development to save
time or because you don't feel ready to
do a bigger investment, it starts to
introduce that erosion and you start
to see the pain. And generally when you
start to feel the pain, it's too late.
And in order to change things, it's
going to require a major change. So not
really easy to deal with.
And the goal here of a good architecture
is really that it should be easily
understood by everybody. It should be
modular, and having that health in your
architecture will really be the only way
you can maintain long-term speed,
long-term velocity in your development.
So this is really the problem we are
trying to solve with this new capability
in SonarQube.
So now, solution. I'm going to go
through the solution philosophically,
but also in terms of concepts. So first
let me start a little bit with concepts.
So what is architecture?
And architecture we divide into three.
One is what is the structure of your
application, which is what are the
components and how are they organized,
in other words, what's the hierarchy of
components. That's number one.
Number two is what are the relations
between the components, which is who,
what component has a relation with what
component.
And the third one is really about design,
which is, hey, how should a component
be used by other components? And once
you put these three things together and
you start to manage them together, you
actually manage your architecture.
So what we are doing in SonarQube is we
have introduced three concepts. One is
the current architecture, and the
current architecture is what is the
actual architecture, which is what do
you have today? And we actually compute
this at every analysis of your code.
So we reverse engineer and we present
you with a map of your current
architecture. I'm going to go into this
in more detail in a minute. The second
concept is really the intended
architecture, which is what are you
shooting for? Sometimes it's going to be
like what did we intend to do initially,
sometimes it's what do we intend to do
now. It doesn't really matter where this
is coming from. This is really what you
are trying to achieve. And then the next
thing is about what we call deviations,
which is this is really the difference
between the current architecture and the
intended architecture.
And this is a basis for our solution.
So our philosophy from a product point
of view is that we have not built a
product for architects. We actually
have built a product for teams, which is,
and team actually includes architects,
but this is not it. So it's about
making sure people understand what they
are dealing with, what kind of animal
they are dealing with, with their
application, that they understand how
it's structured, what are the
relationships, etcetera, etcetera.
You can enforce it, and it's also about
remediation, which is what we have tried
to do is to really make things
actionable.
And we have made it so that humans will
decide, and then stakeholders will solve
the problem, which is stakeholder can be
human, can be AI agent, it doesn't
really matter, but humans will decide
for the application. So this is our
philosophy for that new capability.
And the solution is built really on four
pillars. One is we want everyone, so
human or AI agents, to be able to
understand the current architecture. So
everybody should have access, as part
of the live documentation, should have
access to the current architecture so
that they understand what's happening
and we make sure they actually follow
what is there. The second thing is
ability to formalize intended
architecture. And here we, I think, have
done a good job in making it very simple
to understand what's there. The way it
is formalized is very simple to
understand, but it's also very easy to
define and update, create and update
this intended architecture, which is our
approach makes it extremely simple to
define it the first time and to
incrementally improve it in the future.
The third thing is we have a clear way
of prioritizing issues, which means one
issue defined somewhere will not overlap
with another issue. For example, as soon
as we find that there is a structural
issue, we actually ignore all subsequent
relation issues that are due to that
structural issue. So we only report
issues once. And finally, we make this
extremely actionable thanks to SonarQube,
as we leverage all the capabilities:
workflow, quality gate, showing issues
in the code, etcetera, to make it really
actionable when it comes to remediation.
So let me dig a little bit into current
architecture. So the goal here is really
to discover, slash explore, to
understand the current architecture.
So here we have a full capability to see
top level, up, to be able to zoom in,
obviously zoom out, see the relations,
understand the complexity of the
relations, to understand also the
complexity of components, to understand
whether they are highly coupled, whether
they have a low coupling, and to
understand what are the key components.
You will see when you explore your
application that certain components,
they are super key, and should you have
to change them, it would be extremely
difficult. So that's really the goal of
our current architecture.
Then the intended architecture is really
about defining what do you want to put
under control. Intended architecture
should not map your entire app. It
doesn't make sense if you start to map
your entire app. It means that you're
going to have pretty much to change it
every time you change the app. And this
is not what you want. What you want
there is to decide what's important,
really starting from the top level.
What's important? What do you want to
put under control in terms of
components? And then you define the
relationship between these important
components.
And then it becomes really the blueprint
for all stakeholders. So human, agent,
doesn't matter, this will be their
reference. And every time they drift
from that intended architecture, they
will actually get warnings, it will
break quality gate, etcetera, etcetera.
And how do you define this intended
architecture? We have actually put a few
constraints in place in order to, first,
simplify the way you define architecture,
second, it enables to easily edit what
you have, so be incremental, and thirdly,
it helps to make sure that if three
different people want to define the same
architecture, the definition will be
exactly identical. So there are not
several ways to do the same thing.
So how do we do this? First, you start
top-down, which is you cannot just work
on a specific part of the app. You have
to really define from the top the part
you want to work on. The second thing is,
which is super important, only what you
add will be verified, which is anything
which is not in your intended
architecture will actually be ignored
when we run the analysis. The third
thing is that you can only define
allowed dependencies between components.
You cannot say this relationship is
forbidden. Actually the way to say this
is to not define a dependency.
The fourth thing also, it's in the third
bullet point here, is that this allowed
dependency will only be defined between
siblings, i.e., components that have the
same parent, which is you cannot define
any other type of dependencies, and
dependencies will be inherited by sub
components, which means if you define,
for example in Java, that a relation is
allowed between module A and module B,
it means that every subcomponent of
module A can have a relation with every
component in module B. For now we are
not dealing with design. This is coming
in the next version, and once we deal
with design, we will also be able to say
when you access module B, you can only
access it through XYZ, which are
subcomponents of module B. And finally,
dependencies are not transitive, which
means if module A can have a relation to
module B and module B can have a
relation to module C, it doesn't mean
module A can have a relation with module
C. Actually the opposite, which is
unless you define specifically module A
to module C dependency, it will be
forbidden. So these are the rules that
you have to use to create the intended
architecture, which will make that it
will be uniquely defined. It will be
unique once you define it.
So now let's talk a little bit about the
workflow here. So I mentioned earlier
that as soon as you run an analysis,
you get access to current architecture.
You then can define your intended
architecture, and once you have both,
we are able to generate what we call
architecture issues. Architecture issues
are actually divided into two. One is
deviations, which I mentioned earlier,
which is really the difference between
current architecture and intended
architecture. And the second type of
issues that we generate are what we call
architecture flaws, which is these
issues are generic. They don't really
depend upon your intended architecture.
These are things like tangles, which is
like cycle dependencies between
components, and sometimes tangled cycle
dependencies between components,
oversized components, too much
coupling, low coupling, etcetera,
etcetera. So all of this comes as part
of what we call the architecture flaws.
So flaws plus deviations make
architectural issues, and these issues
get actually converted into what we call
SonarQube issues, which are code-level
issues, which then become what you need
to solve in order to resolve the
architecture issues. Those SonarQube
issues, they are very useful because
they actually show up in existing
dashboards. They will break quality
gate. In the future, they will be shown
in the IDE also, so that it can really
be done really on the left of your
process, to solve it or to not
introduce problems.
Once all of this happens, you will note
that every architecture issue will
actually appear in two forms, in two
shapes. One is architecture issues,
which is really the high-level issue,
which is okay, module A should not have
a relationship to module B will be an
architectural issue, for example. And
then we are going to transform this to
code issues, which is this module A to
module B actually could be broken like
30 times. If that's the case, we will
create 30 code issues, which will really
enable you to track what's the real
debt that you have here and also enable
you to track the progress when you
remediate, which is instead of saying
it's one issue and it's going to take
you weeks to solve, what you can see
here, it's actually 30 code issues and
every time you fix one, you're going to
see the progress. Finally I would like
to say a word about some other work that
we are doing around architecture, which
is we also are working on architecture
as context for AI agents, and this
comes as twofold. One is we are working
on providing the current architecture to
AI agents so that they actually deliver
code that works. Let's say they deliver
more often code that will work better,
so they do a better job basically, and
to deliver that code, AI agents will
also be more effective. I think here
you can do comparison with humans, which
is if tomorrow you have to work on an
app that you don't know and you have no
idea how it's constructed, it's going to
take you a while to figure that out, and
the probability that you actually do a
good job is lower than if you fully know
the architecture. Same for agents. Here
we want to provide this current
architecture as context for agents. The
second part is we want to also provide
intended architecture as context for
agents in order for agents to deliver
something that complies with what we
want them to do, which is basically the
blueprint for architecture. So we are
working on these two things, which
should be delivered pretty soon.
Finally, I would like to talk about now
what's next for you if you want to
engage with this new capability. So for
now architecture is only available in
SonarQube Cloud. It comes as part of all
plans there and it comes at no
additional cost. We are planning to
release SonarQube Server later on this
year. It is available for five main
languages: Java, JavaScript,
TypeScript, Python, and C#.
Again, we are going to add more
languages later on this year. Once you
activate the feature, you will get an
architecture tab, which is the entry
point for architecture. It's a new
dashboard, and you should know that an
analysis is required. So when you make
any change, for any change to be taken
into account, you will need to run an
analysis. Right now we have about
800,000 LOC limit to make sure we have
good performances, and that's about it.
And I just would like to mention that
everything I've described here will be
demoed by Christian J in the next
session. So you will really have the
SonarQube architecture in action in that
other session. Thank you for your
attention.

This presentation is going to be divided into three parts. First I'm going to talk about the problem we are trying to solve. Second part is going to be about the solution, what we have implemented in Sonar to solve this. And the third part is going to be what's next for you. So let me start with the problem. Basically, as you know, the world operates on software, which means that software is now the single most valuable asset for organizations. And one thing which reduces this value is actually technical debt, which I think we have, in the last 17 years, done a very good job to help organizations handle. But we have mostly worked at the code level, which is we have helped people to work on their maintainability, reliability, and security with SonarQube, and we felt it was time to actually move into more structural debt, which is about how the software, how the application, is organized. What are the abstractions? What's the design? Do components have really single responsibility, etcetera, etcetera. And when you do not follow this, it's actually not that much fun, which is you can see symptoms like engineers are leaving because the environment is too messy. Every change you make has side effects and they are very difficult to explain, and overall your velocity is going to drop. So these are the symptoms of not managing architecture. What you should also know is that it doesn't come suddenly. This is very sneaky, the way it arises, and it's very much an erosion, which is generally at software creation, application creation, there is no real problem. People thought about what they want to build. They organize it well, and it's okay. But little by little, when you make shortcuts during development to save time or because you don't feel ready to do a bigger investment, it starts to introduce that erosion and you start to see the pain. And generally when you start to feel the pain, it's too late. And in order to change things, it's going to require a major change. So not really easy to deal with. And the goal here of a good architecture is really that it should be easily understood by everybody. It should be modular, and having that health in your architecture will really be the only way you can maintain long-term speed, long-term velocity in your development. So this is really the problem we are trying to solve with this new capability in SonarQube. So now, solution. I'm going to go through the solution philosophically, but also in terms of concepts. So first let me start a little bit with concepts. So what is architecture? And architecture we divide into three. One is what is the structure of your application, which is what are the components and how are they organized, in other words, what's the hierarchy of components. That's number one. Number two is what are the relations between the components, which is who, what component has a relation with what component. And the third one is really about design, which is, hey, how should a component be used by other components? And once you put these three things together and you start to manage them together, you actually manage your architecture. So what we are doing in SonarQube is we have introduced three concepts. One is the current architecture, and the current architecture is what is the actual architecture, which is what do you have today? And we actually compute this at every analysis of your code. So we reverse engineer and we present you with a map of your current architecture. I'm going to go into this in more detail in a minute. The second concept is really the intended architecture, which is what are you shooting for? Sometimes it's going to be like what did we intend to do initially, sometimes it's what do we intend to do now. It doesn't really matter where this is coming from. This is really what you are trying to achieve. And then the next thing is about what we call deviations, which is this is really the difference between the current architecture and the intended architecture. And this is a basis for our solution. So our philosophy from a product point of view is that we have not built a product for architects. We actually have built a product for teams, which is, and team actually includes architects, but this is not it. So it's about making sure people understand what they are dealing with, what kind of animal they are dealing with, with their application, that they understand how it's structured, what are the relationships, etcetera, etcetera. You can enforce it, and it's also about remediation, which is what we have tried to do is to really make things actionable. And we have made it so that humans will decide, and then stakeholders will solve the problem, which is stakeholder can be human, can be AI agent, it doesn't really matter, but humans will decide for the application. So this is our philosophy for that new capability. And the solution is built really on four pillars. One is we want everyone, so human or AI agents, to be able to understand the current architecture. So everybody should have access, as part of the live documentation, should have access to the current architecture so that they understand what's happening and we make sure they actually follow what is there. The second thing is ability to formalize intended architecture. And here we, I think, have done a good job in making it very simple to understand what's there. The way it is formalized is very simple to understand, but it's also very easy to define and update, create and update this intended architecture, which is our approach makes it extremely simple to define it the first time and to incrementally improve it in the future. The third thing is we have a clear way of prioritizing issues, which means one issue defined somewhere will not overlap with another issue. For example, as soon as we find that there is a structural issue, we actually ignore all subsequent relation issues that are due to that structural issue. So we only report issues once. And finally, we make this extremely actionable thanks to SonarQube, as we leverage all the capabilities: workflow, quality gate, showing issues in the code, etcetera, to make it really actionable when it comes to remediation. So let me dig a little bit into current architecture. So the goal here is really to discover, slash explore, to understand the current architecture. So here we have a full capability to see top level, up, to be able to zoom in, obviously zoom out, see the relations, understand the complexity of the relations, to understand also the complexity of components, to understand whether they are highly coupled, whether they have a low coupling, and to understand what are the key components. You will see when you explore your application that certain components, they are super key, and should you have to change them, it would be extremely difficult. So that's really the goal of our current architecture. Then the intended architecture is really about defining what do you want to put under control. Intended architecture should not map your entire app. It doesn't make sense if you start to map your entire app. It means that you're going to have pretty much to change it every time you change the app. And this is not what you want. What you want there is to decide what's important, really starting from the top level. What's important? What do you want to put under control in terms of components? And then you define the relationship between these important components. And then it becomes really the blueprint for all stakeholders. So human, agent, doesn't matter, this will be their reference. And every time they drift from that intended architecture, they will actually get warnings, it will break quality gate, etcetera, etcetera. And how do you define this intended architecture? We have actually put a few constraints in place in order to, first, simplify the way you define architecture, second, it enables to easily edit what you have, so be incremental, and thirdly, it helps to make sure that if three different people want to define the same architecture, the definition will be exactly identical. So there are not several ways to do the same thing. So how do we do this? First, you start top-down, which is you cannot just work on a specific part of the app. You have to really define from the top the part you want to work on. The second thing is, which is super important, only what you add will be verified, which is anything which is not in your intended architecture will actually be ignored when we run the analysis. The third thing is that you can only define allowed dependencies between components. You cannot say this relationship is forbidden. Actually the way to say this is to not define a dependency. The fourth thing also, it's in the third bullet point here, is that this allowed dependency will only be defined between siblings, i.e., components that have the same parent, which is you cannot define any other type of dependencies, and dependencies will be inherited by sub components, which means if you define, for example in Java, that a relation is allowed between module A and module B, it means that every subcomponent of module A can have a relation with every component in module B. For now we are not dealing with design. This is coming in the next version, and once we deal with design, we will also be able to say when you access module B, you can only access it through XYZ, which are subcomponents of module B. And finally, dependencies are not transitive, which means if module A can have a relation to module B and module B can have a relation to module C, it doesn't mean module A can have a relation with module C. Actually the opposite, which is unless you define specifically module A to module C dependency, it will be forbidden. So these are the rules that you have to use to create the intended architecture, which will make that it will be uniquely defined. It will be unique once you define it. So now let's talk a little bit about the workflow here. So I mentioned earlier that as soon as you run an analysis, you get access to current architecture. You then can define your intended architecture, and once you have both, we are able to generate what we call architecture issues. Architecture issues are actually divided into two. One is deviations, which I mentioned earlier, which is really the difference between current architecture and intended architecture. And the second type of issues that we generate are what we call architecture flaws, which is these issues are generic. They don't really depend upon your intended architecture. These are things like tangles, which is like cycle dependencies between components, and sometimes tangled cycle dependencies between components, oversized components, too much coupling, low coupling, etcetera, etcetera. So all of this comes as part of what we call the architecture flaws. So flaws plus deviations make architectural issues, and these issues get actually converted into what we call SonarQube issues, which are code-level issues, which then become what you need to solve in order to resolve the architecture issues. Those SonarQube issues, they are very useful because they actually show up in existing dashboards. They will break quality gate. In the future, they will be shown in the IDE also, so that it can really be done really on the left of your process, to solve it or to not introduce problems. Once all of this happens, you will note that every architecture issue will actually appear in two forms, in two shapes. One is architecture issues, which is really the high-level issue, which is okay, module A should not have a relationship to module B will be an architectural issue, for example. And then we are going to transform this to code issues, which is this module A to module B actually could be broken like 30 times. If that's the case, we will create 30 code issues, which will really enable you to track what's the real debt that you have here and also enable you to track the progress when you remediate, which is instead of saying it's one issue and it's going to take you weeks to solve, what you can see here, it's actually 30 code issues and every time you fix one, you're going to see the progress. Finally I would like to say a word about some other work that we are doing around architecture, which is we also are working on architecture as context for AI agents, and this comes as twofold. One is we are working on providing the current architecture to AI agents so that they actually deliver code that works. Let's say they deliver more often code that will work better, so they do a better job basically, and to deliver that code, AI agents will also be more effective. I think here you can do comparison with humans, which is if tomorrow you have to work on an app that you don't know and you have no idea how it's constructed, it's going to take you a while to figure that out, and the probability that you actually do a good job is lower than if you fully know the architecture. Same for agents. Here we want to provide this current architecture as context for agents. The second part is we want to also provide intended architecture as context for agents in order for agents to deliver something that complies with what we want them to do, which is basically the blueprint for architecture. So we are working on these two things, which should be delivered pretty soon. Finally, I would like to talk about now what's next for you if you want to engage with this new capability. So for now architecture is only available in SonarQube Cloud. It comes as part of all plans there and it comes at no additional cost. We are planning to release SonarQube Server later on this year. It is available for five main languages: Java, JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, and C#. Again, we are going to add more languages later on this year. Once you activate the feature, you will get an architecture tab, which is the entry point for architecture. It's a new dashboard, and you should know that an analysis is required. So when you make any change, for any change to be taken into account, you will need to run an analysis. Right now we have about 800,000 LOC limit to make sure we have good performances, and that's about it. And I just would like to mention that everything I've described here will be demoed by Christian J in the next session. So you will really have the SonarQube architecture in action in that other session. Thank you for your attention.
